questions.content: After her parents' divorce, Maria continues to maintain a close ____ with both sides of the family, even on awkward holidays.
questions.explanation: rapport = 关系，融洽（B2 词）；指与某人良好的关系。其余三项不是真实词或拼写错误。

questions.content: Although Lily and her cousin live on different continents, they share a strong family ____ and call each other every weekend.
questions.explanation: bond = 纽带、深厚的情感联系（B2）。band 是乐队/带子，bound 是边界/必定，bind 是动词，均不合搭配。

questions.content: Their relationship became ____ after the inheritance dispute; they have not spoken for two years.
questions.explanation: estranged = 疏远的，专门描述家人/伴侣关系破裂（B2）。stranger 是名词陌生人；strange 形容奇怪；strangled 是被勒住，全错。

questions.content: The travel agent advised us to book ____ to avoid the peak-season prices.
questions.explanation: well in advance = 提前许久（固定搭配）。good in advance / early on time / very on time 均不是英语习惯说法，typical PET-FCE bridge collocation 题。

questions.content: The flight was ____ for three hours due to bad weather, so passengers had to wait at the gate.
questions.explanation: delayed (passive) = 被推迟（B1+ 高频）。late 是形容词不能+for 时长；lasted 是动词不及物用法不对；waited 主语应该是人。

questions.content: Make sure your ____ is valid for at least six months before you apply for an international visa.
questions.explanation: passport = 护照（旅行词）。passing 是经过；passenger 是乘客；password 是密码，全是 form-similar 干扰。

questions.content: Her ____ analysis of the historical document impressed the professor and earned her a top grade.
questions.explanation: thorough /ˈθʌrə/ = 彻底的，形容分析深入（B2）。through 介词穿过；though 副词/连词；thought 是想/思想。同形干扰。

questions.content: The teacher emphasised the importance of ____ when writing academic essays — every claim must be supported by evidence.
questions.explanation: rigour (n.) = 严谨（B2 抽象名词）。rigid 是形容词僵硬；rigorous 是形容词严格的；rig 是装备/操控。

questions.content: After failing the exam twice, he decided to ____ to a tutor to help him prepare for the retake.
questions.explanation: turn to (sb) = 求助于某人（PET-FCE 高频 phrasal verb）。turn over = 翻转；turn off = 关闭；turn into = 变成。意思都不对。

questions.content: The restaurant is famous for its ____ menu — every dish is made with locally grown organic ingredients.
questions.explanation: seasonal = 应季的（描述菜单）。seasoning 是调料名词；season 是名词季节；seasoned (adj) 是有经验的或腌制过的，但搭配 menu 不自然。

questions.content: These supplements are rich in ____ that help maintain healthy skin and a strong immune system.
questions.explanation: nutrients (n., 复数) = 营养素（具体物质）。nutrition (n.) 是抽象的营养概念；nutritional / nutritious 是形容词，不能作 of 后宾语。

questions.content: Photography became more than a hobby for him — it gradually ____ into a full-time career.
questions.explanation: evolve into = 演变为（B2）。envy 是嫉妒（不及物形不接 into）；envisage 是设想；envoyed 不是动词。

questions.content: Whenever she has free time, she ____ herself in painting and forgets about everything else.
questions.explanation: immerse oneself in = 沉浸于（B2 reflexive）。imitate 是模仿；impact 是影响；impress 是给…留下印象。

questions.content: The shop offers a full ____ within 30 days if the product turns out to be defective.
questions.explanation: refund = 退款（B1+ 商务词）。refer 是参考；refuse 是拒绝；refurbish 是翻新。同前缀 form-similar 干扰。

questions.content: I cannot ____ to buy that designer bag this month; my rent has already taken most of my salary.
questions.explanation: can afford to do = 买得起（虽然题目里 'afford' 直接接 to-V 是常见考点；选项 A 'afford' 在 'cannot afford to buy' 中代表 'afford' 后接 'to buy'，A 正确）。C 重复 to 与原句 to 冲突；D afford in 不存在。'effort' 是名词不接 to 不定式动作。

questions.content: The committee took the criticism into ____ before finalising its proposal.
questions.explanation: take sth into account = 考虑（FCE 高频 collocation）。accord 是协议；accuracy 是准确度；acceptance 是接受 — 都不能与 take into 搭配成此意。

questions.content: Her résumé does not really ____ to the requirements of this senior position.
questions.explanation: measure up to = 达到（标准、要求）。match up 通常匹配某物；size up 是评估某人/形势；weigh up 是权衡。同义 phrasal verb 辨析。

questions.content: The new evidence sheds ____ on the mysterious disappearance of the documents.
questions.explanation: shed light on = 阐明、揭示（FCE 固定搭配）。其余名词与动词 shed 不构成习语。

questions.content: It would be ____ to assume that the project will finish on time without any delay.
questions.explanation: naïve = 天真的（FCE 抽象 adj，与 to assume 搭配）。negative 是负面的；neat 是整洁；needy 是贫困的。

questions.content: After hours of debate, the board members finally reached a ____ decision.
questions.explanation: unanimous = 全票通过的（B2 collocation 'unanimous decision/vote'）。uniform 是统一的（形式）；unique 是独特的；unilateral 是单方面的，正反相反。

questions.content: Despite long hours, she remained ____ to delivering high-quality work for her team.
questions.explanation: be committed to (doing) = 致力于（FCE 高频）。commence 是开始；comment 是评论；commission 是委托。同前缀 form-similar 干扰。

questions.content: The company's recent decision to ____ its overseas branch surprised many employees.
questions.explanation: downsize = 裁员/缩小规模（FCE 商务词）。downturn 是经济下滑名词；download 是下载；downgrade 是降级。

questions.content: Modern smartphones are ____ with advanced facial-recognition systems for added security.
questions.explanation: be equipped with = 配备（FCE 高频）。equate 是等同；equivalent 是相等的；equivocal 是模棱两可的。

questions.content: The new app allows users to ____ their daily steps and monitor their fitness goals.
questions.explanation: track = 追踪（持续、可视）— 与 fitness 搭配最自然。trace 是查询源头；trail 是追踪线索；trick 是欺骗。

questions.content: After three weeks of intensive training, she experienced a noticeable ____ in her stamina.
questions.explanation: boost = 提升（与 stamina/energy 搭配）。boast 是吹嘘；burst 是爆发；blast 是猛烈攻击/响声。form-similar 一字之差。

questions.content: The doctor advised him to ____ on processed food and eat more fresh vegetables.
questions.explanation: cut down on = 减少摄入（FCE phrasal verb）。cut out = 完全戒除（更强但 'cut out on' 不对，而是 'cut out X'）；cut off = 切断；cut up = 切碎。

questions.content: Many endangered species are on the ____ of extinction unless conservation efforts succeed.
questions.explanation: on the brink of = 处于…的边缘（FCE 习语，常与 extinction/disaster 搭配）。break/branch/brand 全错。

questions.content: Plastic waste is having a ____ impact on marine life around the world.
questions.explanation: devastating = 毁灭性的（与 impact 搭配，B2）。delicate 是精致的；delightful 是令人愉快的；delicious 是美味的。

questions.content: The documentary ____ a thought-provoking debate about social media addiction.
questions.explanation: sparked a debate = 引发辩论（FCE 高频 collocation）。sparkle 是闪烁；spread 是扩散（不接 debate as 自动词）；spelled 是拼写。

questions.content: The new park is intended to ____ a sense of community among local residents.
questions.explanation: foster a sense of = 培养某种感觉（B2 抽象搭配）。form 是形成（具体）；force 是强迫；forge 是锻造（forge bonds 也行但与 a sense of 不自然）。

questions.content: The novel raises profound questions about the ____ of human existence in a digital age.
questions.explanation: essence = 本质（C1 抽象名词）。absence 是缺席；audience 是观众；evidence 是证据。

questions.content: The philosopher argued that true ____ cannot be achieved without freedom of thought.
questions.explanation: enlightenment = 启蒙、开悟（C1 抽象）。entitlement 是权利；entanglement 是纠缠；enrolment 是注册。

questions.content: The government has been criticised for failing to address the widening ____ between rich and poor.
questions.explanation: disparity = 不平等差距（C1 social-issues 词）。departure 是离开；dispute 是争论；display 是展示。

questions.content: Many cities have introduced new policies to combat ____ and provide shelter for those in need.
questions.explanation: homelessness = 无家可归现象（C1 抽象社会词）。homeland 是祖国；homeward 是向家方向 adv；homely 是朴实的。

questions.content: AI systems can ____ vast amounts of data far more quickly than any human researcher could.
questions.explanation: process data = 处理数据（CAE tech 高频搭配）。progress 是进步（不及物，不接 data）；proceed 是继续；produce 是生产。pro- 前缀 form-similar 陷阱。

questions.content: ____ you finish the report by Friday, the manager will not be pleased.
questions.explanation: unless = if not（条件句 type 1 的否定形式）。'If you finish' 表示 if 完成 — 与后半句逻辑相反；whether 表示是否；provided that 表条件但缺 that，而且语义为 '只要…就' 与原句相反。

questions.content: If the system ____ a critical error, it automatically sends an alert to the technical team.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional 一般规律：If + Present Simple, Present Simple. detects 与主语 system 主谓一致。其余形式破坏 zero conditional 结构。

questions.content: She is not at her desk and the lights are off — she ____ have already left for the day.
questions.explanation: must have done 表示对过去事实的较强推测。'can have done' 通常用于否定/疑问；'should have done' 表示本应该（含责备）；'would have' 是虚拟语气。

questions.content: You ____ have told me earlier — I made plans for tonight without knowing about the meeting.
questions.explanation: could have told 表示遗憾/责备：'你本来可以告诉我'。must have told 是表强烈推测；may have 表轻微可能；can have 通常仅疑问/否定。

questions.content: The more you practise, ____ you will become at solving these problems.
questions.explanation: 'the more..., the more/better...' 双比较结构（the + 比较级…the + 比较级…）。其余形式破坏结构。

questions.content: Of all the candidates I have interviewed today, she is by ____ the most qualified.
questions.explanation: by far + 最高级 = 表强调（远远是…）。'much' 修饰比较级 (much better) 但不直接修饰最高级 'most'；'more' 是比较级形式；'very' 不与 by 连用。

questions.content: While I ____ a shower, the postman delivered a parcel that I had been expecting for weeks.
questions.explanation: Past Continuous (was having) 表示过去某动作进行中被另一短动作 (delivered) 打断 — 经典 'while X-ing, Y happened'。had 是 simple past；其余两项是 perfect。

questions.content: I cannot believe how much this neighbourhood ____ since I moved away ten years ago.
questions.explanation: Present Perfect 'has changed' 强调从过去到现在的变化结果，与 'since + 过去时间' 搭配。simple past 不能与 since + ago 一起；present continuous 不表完成；past perfect 需更早参考点。

questions.content: ____ you ever ____ to the new restaurant downtown? I heard the desserts are amazing.
questions.explanation: Have you ever been to + 地方 = 生活经验（你去过…吗？）。'Have you ever gone to' 通常表示已经走了未回来；其余两个时态错。

questions.content: She ____ for the same company for fifteen years before she finally decided to start her own business.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect Continuous (had been working) 表示在另一过去动作 (decided) 之前持续了一段时间。Present Perfect Continuous 'has been' 主参考点是现在，与 'before she decided'（过去）冲突。

questions.content: My eyes are tired because I ____ at the screen all morning without a break.
questions.explanation: Present Perfect Continuous 强调持续到现在的动作产生了现在的结果（眼睛累）。simple past 切断与现在的联系；continuous 仅描述当前；past perfect 缺过去参考点。

questions.content: How long ____ that book? I noticed you started it last month.
questions.explanation: 持续动作 'How long have you been V-ing' (是否还在进行)。'have you read' 询问完成度而非过程；其余时态错。

questions.content: By the time the rescue team arrived, the climbers ____ for over six hours in freezing temperatures.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect Continuous (had been V-ing) 表示在另一过去动作（arrived）之前持续了一段时间。Past Continuous 仅描述同时；present perfect 不与过去时间一起；simple past 不强调持续。

questions.content: She realised she ____ her keys in the office, so she had to take a taxi back to retrieve them.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect (had left) 表示在 'realised' 之前已发生的动作。simple past 难以表先后；present perfect 与过去时间不符；past continuous 表进行。

questions.content: Until that morning, I ____ such a stunning sunrise over the mountains.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect (had never seen) 表示在 'that morning' 之前的所有时间未曾见过。'have never seen' 现在完成不能与 'until that morning' 配；simple past 错时序；continuous 罕见。

questions.content: The garden was a mess because nobody ____ care of it for several months.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect Continuous (had been taking care) 表示在过去时间点之前持续多月没人照看 — 与结果 'was a mess' 搭配。simple past 不强调持续；past continuous 描述当时；present perfect 与过去时态不符。

questions.content: When the police finally caught him, he ____ from the law for over two years.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect Continuous 强调过去某时点之前持续多年的动作。'had been hiding' 与 'for over two years' 搭配最佳。

questions.content: If I ____ harder for the entrance exam, I would be studying at a top university now.
questions.explanation: Mixed conditional：过去未做（had + p.p.）→ 影响现在（would be）。'studied' 是 type 2（同时假设），但因主句 'now' 现在结果且条件是过去，必须用 type 3 在 if 子句。

questions.content: If she ____ the meeting, she would have known about the new policy changes.
questions.explanation: Type 3 conditional：If + had + p.p., would have + p.p.（与过去事实相反）。simple past 是 type 2；其余形式错。

questions.content: If I were you, I ____ that suspicious-looking email before opening any attachments.
questions.explanation: Type 2 conditional：If + were/Verb-ed, would + V（与现在事实相反）。'will' 是 type 1；'would have deleted' 是 type 3。

questions.content: Had she known about the traffic, she ____ an earlier train.
questions.explanation: Inverted type 3 conditional：'Had + S + p.p.' = 'If she had known'。主句 would have + p.p.。其余时态破坏 type 3 结构。

questions.knowledge_point: Grammar/Modals Perfect (could have / should have / must have)
questions.content: The lights are on but no one is answering the door — they ____ home and forgotten about our visit.
questions.explanation: must have + p.p. = 强烈推测过去（一定已经…）。'should have gone' 表本应该；'could go' 是现在能力；'may go' 是当下推测。

questions.knowledge_point: Grammar/Modals Perfect (could have / should have / must have)
questions.content: You ____ told me about the meeting earlier — I had already accepted another invitation.
questions.explanation: should have + p.p. = 本应该（带责备）。must 表强烈推测；would have 表虚拟；may have 表轻微推测。

questions.knowledge_point: Grammar/Modals Perfect (could have / should have / must have)
questions.content: She ____ won the competition if she had practised more, but she did not put in enough effort.
questions.explanation: could have + p.p. 在 type-3 主句中表示 '本来可以但没有做到'。'must have' 表必然；'may have' 表轻微可能；'would have to' 是不得不。

questions.content: The new bridge ____ before next summer, according to the construction company.
questions.explanation: Future Perfect Passive: will have been + p.p. — '在 next summer 之前会被建好'。其余形式或主动或时态错。

questions.content: While the documents ____ , the meeting cannot proceed.
questions.explanation: Present Continuous Passive: are being + p.p.（文件正在被准备）。'prepare' 是主动动词；'are preparing' 是主动进行；'have prepared' 主动完成。

questions.content: By the time we arrived at the cinema, the film ____ for almost twenty minutes.
questions.explanation: 电影 '在放映' (主动持续) 用 had been showing — 注意 'show' 在表示 '上映' 时常用主动 (the film was showing); for + 时长 + past perfect cont。'had been shown' 是被动完成（强调被放映过几次），不符语境持续。

questions.content: Several scientific papers ____ on this topic over the past decade.
questions.explanation: Present Perfect Passive: have been + p.p.（论文被发表）— 'over the past decade' 与现在完成搭配。其余主动或时态错。

questions.content: He said, 'I will call you tomorrow.' → He said he ____ me ____.
options: A. would call / the next day
questions.explanation: Reported speech 时态后移：will → would；时间状语 tomorrow → the next day / the following day。其余形式至少一处错。

questions.content: She asked, 'Are you coming to the party?' → She asked ____.
options: A. if I was coming to the party
B. if was I coming to the party
C. if am I coming to the party
D. that I was coming to the party
questions.explanation: 间接一般疑问句结构：ask + if + S + V（陈述语序，时态后移）。其余结构破坏陈述语序或缺 if。

questions.content: 'Don't open the box,' he warned her. → He warned her ____ the box.
questions.explanation: warn / tell sb (not) to do — 否定不定式：not to + V，否定 not 在 to 前。

questions.content: He admitted, 'I have made a mistake.' → He admitted ____ a mistake.
options: C. that he had made it
questions.explanation: admit + V-ing 或 admit + that + 句。'admitted having made' 是 admit + perfect gerund 表过去动作。'to make' 错；'that he had made it' 多了 it；'make' 缺时态。

questions.content: The novel, ____ won the Booker Prize last year, has been translated into 30 languages.
questions.explanation: 非限定性定语从句修饰 specific 名词，必须用 which (不能用 that)。what 不引导定语从句；where 修饰地点。

questions.content: Do you remember the woman ____ husband works at the hospital?
questions.explanation: whose 表示所属（这位女士的丈夫…）。who/whom 是主格/宾格；which 不修饰人。

questions.content: This is the café ____ we first met five years ago.
questions.explanation: where 引导限定地点定语从句（先行词 café 是地点）。which/that 后需在子句中作主/宾语 — 此句 met 已不缺成分；when 引导时间。

questions.content: ____ working extra hours every week, he still failed to meet the deadline.
questions.explanation: Despite + V-ing / N (介词)。Although + 句；However 是副词；Whereas 引导对比句。

questions.content: The first applicant has more experience; ____ , the second seems more enthusiastic.
questions.explanation: however 在两句之间用，作副词，置于句首并以逗号隔开。despite 是介词；although 是连词不能独立断句；whereas 通常做状语从句连词。

questions.content: He prefers tea, ____ his sister cannot start her morning without coffee.
questions.explanation: whereas = 然而、相比之下（对比）。despite 是介词；although 表让步而非纯对比；however 在此位置语法不顺（need 句号或分号）。

questions.content: We need to ____ the meeting because the manager has fallen ill.
questions.explanation: call off = 取消（FCE 高频）。call up 是打电话；call out 是大声叫；call on 是拜访 / 号召。

questions.content: Could you ____ the new intern when she arrives tomorrow?
questions.explanation: show sb around = 带某人参观介绍。show off 是炫耀；show up 是露面；show in 是引导某人入内（较老）。

questions.content: The salary is low, but the benefits more than ____ for it.
questions.explanation: make up for = 弥补（FCE 高频 phrasal verb）。take up 是占用；set up 是建立；give up 是放弃。

questions.content: By this time next year, I ____ in this city for over a decade.
questions.explanation: Future Perfect: will have + p.p. — 'by this time next year' 与未来完成搭配。其余时态错或主语-时间不符。

questions.content: Don't call me at 8 pm tomorrow — I ____ a presentation to senior management.
questions.explanation: Future Continuous: will be + V-ing — 表示明天某具体时刻正在做某事。simple future 没强调进行性；present simple/cont 不指未来明日具体时刻。

questions.content: ____ have I encountered such a thoughtful and well-researched proposal.
questions.explanation: Rarely 置于句首 → 主谓倒装 (have I encountered)。'Hardly' 也可句首倒装但通常 'Hardly had S done... when... '；'Rare/Hard' 是形容词不引导倒装。

questions.content: ____ did I realise the importance of saving until I lost my job.
questions.explanation: Only + 时间状语置于句首 → 倒装：'Only then did I realise'。其余结构非标准倒装。

questions.content: The committee insisted that every applicant ____ a personal interview before the final decision.
questions.explanation: Mandative subjunctive: insist that + S + (should) + V（动词原形）。'attends' 是 third-person -s；'attended' 是过去式；'will attend' 加 will 不符虚拟语气。

questions.knowledge_point: Grammar/Cleft Sentences (It is X that... / What X is...)
questions.content: ____ encouragement that helped her finish her dissertation, not pressure.
options: B. It was that her supervisor's
questions.explanation: 强调句结构：It was + 强调成分 + that + 句。其余三项破坏 cleft 结构。

questions.content: I really regret ____ to her without thinking; I should have stayed calm.
questions.explanation: regret + V-ing = 后悔做过的事（speaking 表过去动作）。'regret to speak' = 遗憾地告诉（书面体）；'having spoken' 也表过去但 regret 习惯用 V-ing；'to have spoken' 不用于 regret。

questions.content: ____ the contract carefully, she finally agreed to sign.
questions.explanation: Perfect participle (Having + p.p.) 表示先后顺序：先审完，再签字。Reviewing 表示同时；Reviewed 是被动；To review 是不定式表目的。

questions.content: If she ____ what was about to happen, she ____ the alarm bell immediately.
options: A. had known / would have rung
questions.explanation: 完整 type 3 mixed: If + had + p.p., would have + p.p.（过去与过去事实相反）。其他选项混杂 type 2 / type 3 不一致。

questions.content:
The Hidden Cost of Convenience

Online shopping has transformed the way people buy almost everything, from groceries to furniture. According to a recent industry report, more than 70 percent of consumers in major cities now order at least one parcel a week, and many would struggle to imagine life without same-day delivery. On the surface, this seems like progress: shoppers save time, retailers reach a wider audience, and small businesses can sell internationally without ever opening a physical store.

Yet the convenience has a darker side. Each parcel takes a journey that begins long before the customer clicks 'pay'. Massive warehouses, often built on the edges of cities, run twenty-four hours a day. Workers there walk dozens of kilometres each shift, picking and packing items at a pace dictated by software rather than human judgment. Whistle-blowers have described how short toilet breaks, ranked productivity targets and short-term contracts combine to produce stress levels not previously associated with retail work.

Environmental consequences are also harder to ignore. Although companies advertise 'eco-friendly' packaging, the carbon footprint of constant fast deliveries — frequently in half-empty vans — is significant. A study by an independent research group found that doorstep delivery of a single book can produce up to four times the emissions of a customer walking to a local bookshop. When customers return items they did not really want — a quarter of clothing purchases on average — those products are sometimes destroyed rather than restocked, simply because the cost of inspection is higher than the value of the item.

There are signs that consumers are beginning to think twice. Some now choose 'no-rush' delivery options, even when faster shipping is free. Others have started 'one-in-one-out' rules at home: a new purchase only enters the household if an old item leaves it. A small but vocal minority have committed to buying nothing new for a whole year, repairing or borrowing instead.

Critics call such efforts symbolic at best. They argue that systemic change requires regulation, not individual willpower. Governments could, for example, introduce a small surcharge on every fast delivery to subsidise greener alternatives, or require clear labelling that shows the true environmental cost of each option. Without such measures, they warn, consumer guilt will simply become the next thing the industry markets to us — sustainability sold as another form of luxury.

What seems certain is that the era of guilt-free clicking is ending. Whether the next stage of e-commerce is shaped by regulation, by individual choices, or — most likely — by some combination of the two, will determine whether convenience can be reconciled with the wider costs we are now beginning to count.

Question 1: Which of the following best summarises the writer's main point?
options: A. Convenient online shopping carries hidden costs that consumers and regulators are starting to address.
B. Online shopping is more environmentally friendly than visiting local stores.
C. Most warehouse workers are satisfied with their working conditions despite the pace.
D. Government regulation has already eliminated the worst problems of e-commerce.
questions.explanation: 全文围绕便利购物的隐藏代价（劳工、环境、退货）以及消费者与监管初步反应展开。B/C/D 与文中信息相反或被否定。

questions.content:
The Quiet Revolution in Sleep Science

For decades, sleep was treated as a kind of biological down time — a passive state in which the brain simply switched off until the morning. Recent neuroscience has overturned that view almost completely. Far from being inactive, the sleeping brain is now understood to be one of the most metabolically demanding organs in the body, performing tasks that wakeful consciousness simply cannot.

The most striking discovery has come from work on the so-called glymphatic system, a kind of nightly cleaning service for the brain. During deep sleep, the spaces between brain cells widen, allowing cerebrospinal fluid to flow more freely and wash away toxic by-products that accumulate during the day. Among these by-products are proteins associated with Alzheimer's disease. Although the long-term link between sleep loss and dementia is still being mapped, several major studies have shown that even one night of poor sleep raises measurable levels of these proteins in the bloodstream.

Memory consolidation, too, depends heavily on sleep. Researchers using brain imaging have shown that the patterns of neural firing during certain sleep stages closely mirror those produced earlier in the day during a learning task — as if the brain were rehearsing what it had just learned. Volunteers deprived of sleep after studying perform significantly worse on tests than those allowed to rest. The effect is so reliable that some educational researchers have called for school start times to be moved back, especially for adolescents whose internal clocks naturally run later.

Not everyone is enthusiastic about every claim, however. Some sleep scientists worry that public messaging has become alarmist. Headlines suggesting that any deviation from a perfect eight hours leads to disaster, they argue, can themselves cause stress-related insomnia. The truth is more nuanced: total sleep need varies, with most adults thriving on between seven and nine hours, while a small minority genuinely function well on less. Quality, regularity and timing seem to matter at least as much as raw duration.

What has united the field, though, is a sense that sleep deserves the same kind of public attention as diet or exercise. Workplaces are starting to experiment with later starts for shift workers, hospitals with quieter wards, and city councils with light-pollution rules in residential zones. Whether these changes will survive economic pressures remains to be seen. But the era when sleep could be skipped without consequence — celebrated, even, as a sign of dedication — is rapidly ending.

Question 1: What does the writer imply about earlier views of sleep?
options: A. They underestimated the brain's activity during the night.
B. They focused too much on memory consolidation.
C. They were largely correct except for one detail.
D. They were based on extensive imaging studies.
questions.explanation: 段 1 'sleep was treated as ... passive ... switched off' 与现在 'one of the most metabolically demanding' 形成对比，暗示之前低估了大脑活跃度。

questions.content:
The Last Bookshop on Maple Street

When Mrs Patel finally turned the key in the lock for the last time, she did so with a small, dry smile that the regulars knew well. It was the same smile she gave when a customer asked for a book she did not stock, or when one of the part-time students dropped a stack of paperbacks. To anyone observing from the pavement, it might have looked like satisfaction. To those who had spent enough Saturdays inside the shop, it looked like something else entirely — a way of refusing to make a fuss.

The shop had survived two recessions, three changes in the rent agreement, and the arrival of an enormous chain bookstore three streets away. What had finally tipped it over the edge was, of all things, a freshly painted bicycle lane. The lane removed the loading bay outside the shop. New parcel deliveries now had to be made on foot, several blocks from the nearest stopping point, and the elderly delivery driver had quietly explained that he could not keep doing this for much longer. From there, the dominoes had fallen.

Some of the regulars had tried to organise a campaign. Posters appeared in the windows of nearby cafés, and a petition collected almost a thousand signatures. The local council had been polite. They had also been firm: the cycling network was a strategic priority. Mrs Patel had been gracious throughout, and had even baked biscuits for the volunteers. Privately, she suspected that the bookshop's fate had been sealed long before the bicycle lane appeared. The lane was simply the last sentence in a much longer story.

It would be easy, perhaps too easy, to read this story as one about town planning, or about the cruelty of large chains, or about the apparent decline of reading itself. None of those readings is exactly wrong. But Mrs Patel, who had given over three decades of her life to choosing books for other people, would have rejected each as too neat. She had always insisted that bookshops were places, first of all, where strangers became briefly familiar. The shelves were the excuse; the conversations were the point.

That, more than the closure itself, is what those who knew the shop will struggle to replace. There are still chain bookstores, and there is, of course, the internet, with its almost limitless catalogue. What is in shorter supply is the unhurried attention of a knowledgeable adult who has read most of what she sells, and who is willing to be wrong with you about a novel for ten minutes on a wet afternoon.

Question 1: What does Mrs Patel's 'small, dry smile' most likely indicate?
options: A. She does not want to draw attention to her sadness.
B. She is genuinely satisfied with the closing of the shop.
C. She is embarrassed by the regulars watching her.
D. She feels triumphant about defeating the council.
questions.explanation: 段 1 直接说明 'a way of refusing to make a fuss' — 不让人察觉难过。其余选项与下文情感（耐心、风度）矛盾。

C. A sudden rise in rent during the third agreement change.
D. Mrs Patel's decision to retire after a long career.

questions.content:
The Last Bookshop on Maple Street

When Mrs Patel finally turned the key in the lock for the last time, she did so with a small, dry smile that the regulars knew well. It was the same smile she gave when a customer asked for a book she did not stock, or when one of the part-time students dropped a stack of paperbacks. To anyone observing from the pavement, it might have looked like satisfaction. To those who had spent enough Saturdays inside the shop, it looked like something else entirely — a way of refusing to make a fuss.

The shop had survived two recessions, three changes in the rent agreement, and the arrival of an enormous chain bookstore three streets away. What had finally tipped it over the edge was, of all things, a freshly painted bicycle lane. The lane removed the loading bay outside the shop. New parcel deliveries now had to be made on foot, several blocks from the nearest stopping point, and the elderly delivery driver had quietly explained that he could not keep doing this for much longer. From there, the dominoes had fallen.

Some of the regulars had tried to organise a campaign. Posters appeared in the windows of nearby cafés, and a petition collected almost a thousand signatures. The local council had been polite. They had also been firm: the cycling network was a strategic priority. Mrs Patel had been gracious throughout, and had even baked biscuits for the volunteers. Privately, she suspected that the bookshop's fate had been sealed long before the bicycle lane appeared. The lane was simply the last sentence in a much longer story.

It would be easy, perhaps too easy, to read this story as one about town planning, or about the cruelty of large chains, or about the apparent decline of reading itself. None of those readings is exactly wrong. But Mrs Patel, who had given over three decades of her life to choosing books for other people, would have rejected each as too neat. She had always insisted that bookshops were places, first of all, where strangers became briefly familiar. The shelves were the excuse; the conversations were the point.

That, more than the closure itself, is what those who knew the shop will struggle to replace. There are still chain bookstores, and there is, of course, the internet, with its almost limitless catalogue. What is in shorter supply is the unhurried attention of a knowledgeable adult who has read most of what she sells, and who is willing to be wrong with you about a novel for ten minutes on a wet afternoon.

Question 3: Which statement best captures the irony in paragraph 2?
options: A. A measure designed to make the city better quietly destroys a beloved local institution.
B. Recessions were less harmful than chain stores.
C. The delivery driver was secretly working for a competitor.
D. Mrs Patel had personally requested the bicycle lane.
questions.explanation: 讽刺在于：自行车道（公益改善）反而毁了书店。其余 B/C/D 与原文不符。

questions.content:
Four Career Changers Tell Their Stories

A. Mei (former lawyer, now ceramics teacher)
For a long time I told myself that the discomfort I felt at work was just part of being an adult. Eventually I admitted it was not. The shift to teaching ceramics was, on paper, a financial disaster. In practice, it was the first thing in years I did not have to brace myself for on Sunday evening. I do still miss the structure of the firm, and I am genuinely surprised by how often I think of my old colleagues. But I have stopped looking for a single perfect job; I have arranged my week so that no day is unbearable, and that turns out to be enough.

B. Sven (former software engineer, now bakery co-owner)
The decision was less brave than people assume. I had been baking bread on weekends for five years before I left my job, and the queues at the local market kept getting longer. My partner and I spent eighteen months building a financial cushion before we signed the lease, and we still had to borrow from his parents in the second winter. What surprises me most is not the workload, which I had expected, but how much of running a small business is paperwork. I tell people who ask: do not romanticise it.

C. Adaeze (former marketing manager, now NHS nurse)
I was forty-two when I started my nursing degree, and I will be the first to say I had no idea what I was getting into. The first six months on a real ward broke me down in ways I am still putting back together. But there is a kind of immediate usefulness in nursing that no marketing campaign ever gave me, and I am protective of that feeling now. Money is much tighter than it was. Friends who knew me 'before' sometimes look at me with what I think is concern; I have stopped trying to convince them.

D. Toby (former school teacher, now freelance translator)
I loved teaching, but my health did not. After a long period of insomnia and migraines, my doctor suggested in the gentlest possible terms that the classroom and I might no longer be a good match. Translation was not a passion; it was a skill I had developed slowly, alongside teaching, and that I knew I could rely on. People sometimes assume the change must have been emotionally devastating. The truth is more boring: I felt enormous relief, and a small but persistent guilt that I had not made the move sooner.

Question 1: Which person describes feeling that the change was less dramatic than others assume?
questions.explanation: B 段 Sven 'less brave than people assume' 明文。其余三人未表达此意。

questions.content:
Two Reviews of the Novel 'Northern Light'

Review A — Critic Helena Cross
'Northern Light' is the kind of novel that, on first reading, feels entirely effortless. Within fifty pages, an entire community has been built up so quietly that you have to remind yourself the place is invented. Vasquez's prose is unfashionably calm: long sentences, generous descriptions of weather, very little plot of the kind a film studio would want. Yet by the final chapter you realise how carefully it has been arranged. The tension is structural rather than scene-by-scene.

Where I disagree with the early reviews is on the ending. Several critics have called it abrupt, almost rude. To me, it is exactly right. A more conventional resolution would have flattered the reader at the cost of the characters. Vasquez has the courage — rare in a debut — to let her people remain unfinished. There are passages in the middle that I wished had been tightened, and the third sister never quite came alive for me. But these are small criticisms in a book of unusual confidence and grace.

Review B — Critic Daniel Ofor
There is, I will concede, a great deal of intelligence in 'Northern Light'. Vasquez clearly knows her village, her sisters, her decade. The descriptions of the moor in winter are genuinely fine. What I cannot accept is the suggestion, made by some otherwise sensible reviewers, that the novel's slowness is a virtue. There is an enormous difference between patience and self-indulgence, and after a while it became hard to tell which I was reading.

The ending, often praised for its restraint, struck me less as courageous than evasive. Three hundred careful pages set up a moral question that is then waved away in two paragraphs. The third sister, in particular, deserved better; she is the most interesting character in the book and remains a sketch. I do not doubt that Vasquez will write a stronger novel; I am simply not persuaded that she has yet.

Question 1: On which point do the two critics most clearly agree?
options: A. Vasquez has produced fine descriptive writing.
B. The ending of the novel is exactly right.
C. The third sister is fully realised as a character.
D. The novel deserves no further attention.
questions.explanation: Helena 'unfashionably calm ... generous descriptions of weather'，Daniel 'descriptions of the moor in winter are genuinely fine' — 两人都肯定描写。其余三项两人观点相反或都未承认。

questions.content:
The Forgotten Pioneer

Long before the language of computing had even been invented, Ada Lovelace was writing what most modern historians now recognise as the world's first algorithm. The setting was unlikely: a small upstairs study in nineteenth-century London, in a household more interested in horse-racing than in mathematics. Lovelace, the only legitimate child of the poet Lord Byron, had been steered firmly away from poetry by her formidable mother — partly out of conviction, partly out of fear that she would inherit her father's instability.

What Lovelace ended up writing instead was a long set of notes appended to her translation of an Italian engineer's paper on Charles Babbage's proposed Analytical Engine. The notes, signed only with her initials, ran to nearly three times the length of the paper itself. Buried within them was a worked example showing how the imagined machine could be used to compute the Bernoulli numbers — a sequence familiar to mathematicians but not, until then, in the hands of any device. It is this passage that twentieth-century scholars would later treat as the first true computer program.

Lovelace's reputation has had a strange afterlife. For most of the hundred years after her early death, she was filed under 'mathematical curiosity', remembered, if at all, as a footnote in Babbage's biography. Then, towards the end of the twentieth century, came a kind of revival. Programming languages were named after her. School textbooks rediscovered her. Companies eager to celebrate women in technology held annual events in her honour.

Yet some historians have begun to push back gently against this neat narrative of recovery. The risk, they argue, is that a complicated and sometimes unhappy figure has been turned into a mascot. Lovelace herself struggled with illness, with the social expectations of an aristocrat, and with the limits of what her collaborator Babbage was willing to share. To turn her into a smiling, uncomplicated icon is to make her, in a different way, invisible again.

The fairest reading, then, may be neither dismissive nor hagiographic. Lovelace did not single-handedly invent computing — Babbage had spent decades developing the machine she was writing about, and an entire intellectual tradition lay behind both. But within the limits of what was possible in her time, she did something genuinely new: she imagined that a calculating machine might one day handle not only numbers, but symbols, music, even thought. The fact that this imagination took place in the corner of a study, between bouts of illness, in a society that did not expect it, is part of why she still matters.

Question 1: Which best summarises the writer's overall view of Ada Lovelace?
options: A. She made a genuinely original contribution while remaining a complex, partly difficult figure.
B. She invented computing on her own and deserves no further reassessment.
C. Her work was largely a footnote in Babbage's much greater achievement.
D. She has been correctly remembered without any distortion.
questions.explanation: 末段总结：non-dismissive non-hagiographic — 既非贬低也非神化。其余 B/C/D 与作者立场冲突。

questions.content:
Should Cities Limit Tourism?

When Venice introduced an entrance fee for day-trippers, reactions were sharply divided. Local residents who had spent years complaining about overcrowded alleys and disturbed sleep tended to welcome the move, while travel companies and a handful of business owners called it an attack on the open city. International commentators, from a comfortable distance, lined up on both sides. Several other historic cities, watching nervously, began consultations of their own.

The debate often gets stuck on a false choice between 'open' and 'closed' cities. In reality, almost every well-functioning city already manages access in some way. Permits regulate film crews, pedestrianised streets exclude cars, museums limit visitor numbers in fragile rooms. The question raised by the Venetian fee is not whether cities should manage flows of people, but what kinds of management are fair and what their wider effects are.

A common argument in favour of fees is that they internalise costs. Tourism brings revenue, but it also creates rubbish, wear-and-tear, and pressure on services. If a small entry fee diverts some of the rubbish budget back from local taxpayers to visitors, the logic is similar to that of a parking charge. Critics, however, point out that fees have an uneven effect: a fee that is trivial to a wealthy traveller may be prohibitive to a school group from a poorer country. The question of who is being kept out matters at least as much as how many.

Other measures attempt to influence behaviour rather than mere numbers. Time-slot booking spreads visits across the day; guided routes steer crowds away from the most fragile lanes; signage in multiple languages reminds visitors of local customs. Where these have been combined sensitively with longer-term housing and transport policies, the result has sometimes been quieter streets and a partial return of younger residents. Where they have been applied as a marketing exercise without structural change, the effect has been mainly cosmetic.

Tourism is too profitable, and travel too widely valued, for any blanket ban to be politically realistic. But the era in which a popular city was simply expected to absorb whatever number of visitors arrived may be drawing to a close. The most important questions facing city authorities are no longer whether to manage tourism, but in whose interest, on what evidence, and at what social cost.

Question 1: What is the main idea of paragraph 2?
options: A. Cities already manage access in many ways; the real issue is what kind of management is fair.
B. Most modern cities are completely open with no restrictions.
C. Pedestrianised streets are the only fair tool of management.
D. Venice's fee was the first attempt to restrict visitors.
questions.explanation: 段 2 用 permits / pedestrianised / museums 例子证明 'almost every well-functioning city already manages access' — A 完全契合。其余 B/C/D 与文中矛盾。

questions.content:
Why We Misjudge Risk

If you ask a group of strangers what the most dangerous activity in their week is, you tend to get a fairly predictable list: flying somewhere on holiday, perhaps; eating in an unfamiliar restaurant; walking home late at night. The actual answer, statistically, is far more boring. Across most of the developed world, the activity most likely to harm an ordinary adult is not flying or food poisoning but driving themselves to work in their own car. The numbers are not even close.

Why does our intuition mislead us so reliably? One reason is the simple bias known to psychologists as 'availability'. Risks that come easily to mind feel bigger than risks that do not, regardless of how often they actually occur. Air crashes are dramatic and widely reported, but they are extremely rare. Routine car journeys are uneventful, but they make up the vast majority of what we actually do. The brain, doing the kind of quick estimation it has evolved to do, prefers vivid stories to dry tables of numbers.

Another factor is control. People generally feel safer in situations they appear to control, even when the appearance is misleading. Driving feels controllable; flying does not. The fact that the drivers around you are tired, distracted, or undertrained is not visible to you in the way that turbulence on a flight suddenly is. The illusion of control is a powerful, and often expensive, comfort.

A third — and perhaps the most subtle — distortion is what behavioural scientists sometimes call 'voluntariness'. We tolerate risks we choose far more readily than risks imposed on us. The same level of harm, presented as a personal decision, looks acceptable; presented as a corporate or government policy, it looks outrageous. Some of this asymmetry is justified — there is real moral weight in choosing for oneself — but a large part is simply emotional.

What can be done about all this? Education helps a little, but only a little. People who can quote the relevant statistics still feel the same instinctive fears. Better-designed defaults seem to do more: clearly labelled risks at the point of decision, simple comparisons rather than long tables, and design choices in transport and medicine that make the safer option also the easier one. The interesting lesson is that we may not be able to think our way out of these biases entirely, but we can sometimes design environments in which they matter less.

Question 1: Which sentence best summarises the article?
options: A. Our risk perception is often biased, but environmental design can mitigate the effects.
B. Our risk perception is generally accurate and matches statistical evidence.
C. Driving is the safest activity once one understands the data.
D. Air crashes are far more common than road accidents.
questions.explanation: 段 1-4 论证 misjudgement，段 5 提出 design 解决。综合即 A。其余三项与文意冲突。

questions.content:
Why We Misjudge Risk

If you ask a group of strangers what the most dangerous activity in their week is, you tend to get a fairly predictable list: flying somewhere on holiday, perhaps; eating in an unfamiliar restaurant; walking home late at night. The actual answer, statistically, is far more boring. Across most of the developed world, the activity most likely to harm an ordinary adult is not flying or food poisoning but driving themselves to work in their own car. The numbers are not even close.

Why does our intuition mislead us so reliably? One reason is the simple bias known to psychologists as 'availability'. Risks that come easily to mind feel bigger than risks that do not, regardless of how often they actually occur. Air crashes are dramatic and widely reported, but they are extremely rare. Routine car journeys are uneventful, but they make up the vast majority of what we actually do. The brain, doing the kind of quick estimation it has evolved to do, prefers vivid stories to dry tables of numbers.

Another factor is control. People generally feel safer in situations they appear to control, even when the appearance is misleading. Driving feels controllable; flying does not. The fact that the drivers around you are tired, distracted, or undertrained is not visible to you in the way that turbulence on a flight suddenly is. The illusion of control is a powerful, and often expensive, comfort.

A third — and perhaps the most subtle — distortion is what behavioural scientists sometimes call 'voluntariness'. We tolerate risks we choose far more readily than risks imposed on us. The same level of harm, presented as a personal decision, looks acceptable; presented as a corporate or government policy, it looks outrageous. Some of this asymmetry is justified — there is real moral weight in choosing for oneself — but a large part is simply emotional.

What can be done about all this? Education helps a little, but only a little. People who can quote the relevant statistics still feel the same instinctive fears. Better-designed defaults seem to do more: clearly labelled risks at the point of decision, simple comparisons rather than long tables, and design choices in transport and medicine that make the safer option also the easier one. The interesting lesson is that we may not be able to think our way out of these biases entirely, but we can sometimes design environments in which they matter less.

Question 3: What is the role of paragraph 3 in the article's argument?
options: A. Introducing a second psychological factor that distorts our risk perception.
B. Refuting the bias described in paragraph 2.
C. Providing the historical origin of car insurance.
D. Concluding the writer's main argument.
questions.explanation: 段 3 开头 'Another factor is control.' 是承接 paragraph 2 的 'One reason' — 第二个因素。

questions.content:
The Slow Comeback of the Public Library

For most of the early 2000s, the public library was an institution that policy-makers found easy to overlook. Reports announced its imminent obsolescence with enough confidence to make the prediction look like fact. Footfall in many branches did indeed fall as digital alternatives expanded; book-borrowing — the traditional measure of success — declined year on year. Several local authorities responded by closing branches outright; others kept them open mainly as community centres with limited hours and shrinking stocks.

The story since has been quieter and more interesting than the obituaries suggested. Visitor numbers have stabilised in many areas, and in some places have begun to climb again. The reason is not, as some optimists claim, that books have made a triumphant return. The reason is that libraries have changed what they are for.

Modern branches host coding clubs for teenagers, English-language sessions for new arrivals, drop-in support for older users navigating online welfare systems, and quiet study spaces for people whose homes are too noisy or too crowded for sustained concentration. Some have launched 'tool libraries', lending out everything from drills to sewing machines. The most innovative branches now define themselves not by their stock of books but by the range of activities they enable.

This shift has not been uncontroversial. Some traditionalists fear that, in becoming everything to everyone, libraries will become nothing in particular. They worry that a public space defined mainly by reading is being slowly replaced by a generic community hub. Others, including many library staff, reply that the older role was always less stable than its defenders remember; libraries have continually reinvented themselves, from elite collections to mass-circulation institutions to today's mixed-use centres.

What seems clear is that, in straitened economic times, the libraries most likely to survive are those that can demonstrate value across multiple dimensions. A branch that supports adult literacy, helps job-seekers prepare applications, gives shelter on a cold afternoon, and still lends books, is harder to defund than one that does only the last of these. The lesson, perhaps, is not that the library has been saved by reinvention, but that it had to reinvent itself in order to be saveable at all.

Question 1: What is the main idea of paragraph 2?
options: A. Library survival is due to a change in their function, not a return of book-borrowing.
B. Books have become more popular than ever in libraries.
C. Libraries have closed almost everywhere.
D. Visitor numbers continue to fall sharply.
questions.explanation: 段 2 直接对比 'not ... books triumphant return' vs 'libraries have changed what they are for' — A 与之一致。其余三项与文相反。

questions.content:
Cities at Night: A New Kind of Pollution

For most of human history, the dark side of a city was just as important as the bright side. Streets were lit, where they were lit at all, by widely spaced gas lamps; whole districts disappeared after sundown, the night sky overhead a familiar map of stars. Today, the experience is utterly different. From any modestly sized city, the Milky Way is invisible. Astronomers, ecologists and an increasing number of urban planners now describe this as light pollution — and the phrase, once unfamiliar, is beginning to enter mainstream policy debate.

The consequences are not merely poetic. Migratory birds, navigating partly by the stars, become disoriented in cities; some species fly into illuminated buildings and die in surprising numbers each year. Insects swarm around bright lamps and exhaust themselves before reaching the dark areas where they would normally pollinate. Hospital studies have begun to link chronic exposure to bright artificial light with disrupted sleep cycles, particularly in children sharing rooms close to floodlit streets or industrial sites.

It is sometimes assumed that brighter is automatically safer. The evidence here is more complicated than the assumption. While well-targeted lighting at junctions and footpaths does seem to reduce certain kinds of accident, blanket flooding of entire neighbourhoods has yielded surprisingly little improvement in crime statistics. Glare from poorly mounted lamps can in fact mask shadows and create temporarily blind areas — exactly the conditions a careful intruder might prefer. Some pilot schemes that simply replaced upward-spilling lamps with carefully shielded fixtures reported lower energy bills and unchanged or even improved safety perceptions among residents.

Resistance to reform tends to come from two directions. Lighting manufacturers, understandably, prefer larger and longer-running installations to smaller, more nuanced ones. Residents themselves, however, also vary. Some cherish the rare view of constellations regained when an unused car-park light is removed; others, accustomed to a permanently lit horizon, find a fully restored night sky strangely threatening at first.

The discussion is not purely about astronomy or aesthetics. As cities continue to expand and energy prices fluctuate, the question of how brightly to light public space is becoming a practical one. The most thoughtful proposals do not aim for a return to gas-lamp dimness; they aim instead for what one writer has called 'directed darkness' — light precisely where it is needed, for the time it is needed, and not a watt more.

Question 1: What is the writer's central claim?
options: A. Light pollution has wide effects, and 'directed darkness' is the sensible response.
B. Cities should immediately switch off all street lighting.
C. Brighter cities are always safer cities.
D. Light pollution mainly concerns astronomers and no one else.
questions.explanation: 末段 'directed darkness' 是核心提案。B 极端；C 文中被否；D 段 1 已扩展到生态/儿童健康。

Question 5: Which statement best captures the writer's tone in the final paragraph?
options: A. Pragmatic — preferring a precise, measured policy over either extreme.
B. Nostalgic — calling for a complete return to nineteenth-century darkness.
C. Indifferent — refusing to take any position.
D. Alarmist — predicting catastrophe in every city within ten years.
questions.explanation: 末段 'do not aim for a return to gas-lamp dimness; they aim instead for ... directed darkness' — 拒绝两极，务实折中。

questions.content:
Five Teachers on Phones in Schools

A. Ms Chen (secondary school, English)
I came to the topic of a phone ban with a lot of skepticism. The pro-ban arguments seemed too neat: lock the phones away, problems solved. After a term of trying it, I am, reluctantly, partly convinced. Lessons are quieter, but more importantly, the breaks are louder — students actually talk to each other in the corridor again. What I would not do is pretend this fixes the deeper issue, which is the way our entire culture treats attention as something that can be infinitely interrupted.

B. Mr Olusanya (primary school, head teacher)
We never allowed phones at our school anyway, so I find some of the recent discussion strange. The interesting question for primary heads is not whether children should bring phones to school. It is whether parents should send them with phones at all at this age. We should be careful not to use school policies as a substitute for that conversation. Schools cannot solve, on their own, what is essentially a question of family habit.

C. Mr Romero (secondary school, mathematics)
Honestly, I supported a ban because the small minority of students who could not put their phones away during lessons were ruining things for everyone. The blanket policy is unfair to the well-behaved majority, but it is more practical than constant individual battles. If you are looking for a principled defense, I am not your man — this was simple triage. I would happily revisit the policy if a more graduated alternative could actually be enforced.

D. Mrs Patel (sixth-form college, sociology)
At the older end of secondary education, I think a strict ban is misguided. Sixth formers will be using phones intensively in further education and at work; we should be teaching them how to manage that, not just removing the device. My current policy: phones face down on the desk, used briefly when relevant. It is not perfect, but it is closer to the world they are about to enter.

E. Mr Singh (secondary school, IT)
I agree with banning phones during lessons but I am wary of moral panic around technology more generally. Phones are not the only way to lose focus, and they will not be the last. We are building a curriculum that, ironically, often demands the very devices we are restricting. The honest answer is to think harder about which tools we use, when, and why — not to wage a war on rectangles.

Question 1: Which teacher mainly justifies the ban as a pragmatic compromise rather than a principled position?
questions.explanation: C 段 'simple triage' / 'If you are looking for a principled defense, I am not your man'。

questions.content:
The Plant Behaviourists

For most of modern biology, the idea that plants might have something resembling behaviour was treated with embarrassment. Plants did not move from place to place; they did not appear to learn; they certainly did not seem to choose. To talk of decision-making in a sunflower was, until quite recently, the kind of thing that ended a young researcher's career before it began.

In the past two decades, however, a small but persistent group of scientists has begun to take such talk seriously. Their argument is not that plants are conscious in the way that humans are. It is, more carefully, that some long-standing definitions of behaviour — adaptive responses to a changing environment, integration of multiple signals, the appearance of preference — apply to plants when one is willing to look closely enough.

Consider, for example, what happens when a young pea plant is placed in a maze with light coming from one direction. The plant reliably grows towards the light, but more strikingly, when given a choice between two arms with different qualities of soil moisture and light, it adjusts its growth in ways that fit the combination of cues rather than any one of them in isolation. Similar studies have shown that some species apparently 'remember' the timing of stress events and adjust their behaviour during the next exposure. None of this requires a brain. It requires only mechanisms, distributed throughout the plant, capable of integrating information.

The pushback has come from two directions. Critics in classical botany worry that loose talk of 'memory' or 'choice' will mislead the public, who already overestimate how much plants resemble animals. Cognitive scientists, meanwhile, are sometimes cautious for the opposite reason: stretching the term 'behaviour' too widely, they say, makes it less useful in animal studies, where it has to do harder work.

Defenders of plant-behaviour research reply that this caution, while reasonable, has often hardened into an unscientific prejudice. The history of biology, they point out, is partly the history of expanding what counts as 'really' alive, intelligent, or social — first to bacteria, then to fungi, then to social insects, and so on. Plants, the largest fraction of the planet's living biomass, may simply be the next category of stubbornly underestimated organisms. Whether that view eventually becomes mainstream depends, perhaps fittingly for plants, less on a single dramatic breakthrough than on slow, accumulating, well-documented growth.

Question 1: Which best summarises the writer's overall view?
options: A. Plant-behaviour research is a credible field that deserves serious consideration despite caution.
B. Plants are conscious in the same way humans are.
C. All claims about plant behaviour are dismissed as pseudoscience.
D. Botany has nothing left to discover.
questions.explanation: 全文承认两面观点，最终偏向认真对待，但保留谨慎语气。其余 B/C/D 走极端。

questions.content:
The Mathematics of Trust

Most accounts of human cooperation focus on the warmer side of the question: kindness, fellow-feeling, the bonds of family or community. Recent research, while not denying these, has shifted attention to something colder. A great deal of what looks like trust between people may, on close examination, be the result of repeated calculation in environments shaped to make cooperation pay.

The starting point of this research is a simple game played in laboratories around the world. Two strangers are paired and given small sums of money. Each can choose, repeatedly, either to share or to keep. Sharing benefits the partner more than it costs you, but only if the partner also shares in return. Long ago, the game was thought to expose human selfishness; with each round, players were predicted to defect. Real human players, however, cooperate far more often than this prediction suggests, especially when they expect to interact again.

That last condition — repeated interaction — turns out to be doing much of the work. Once players know they will face each other again, simple strategies that punish past defection and reward past cooperation outperform almost everything else over time. These strategies, no more complex than 'do unto others as they last did unto you', emerge naturally in groups that play often. They look, on the surface, very much like trust.

The same mathematics applies, in messier form, to whole institutions. Banks that expect to lend to customers many times over their lifetimes behave differently from banks that maximise quarterly fees on one-off transactions. Online marketplaces have learned, sometimes painfully, that visible reputation systems shift behaviour by changing how often a single bad action will be remembered in future ones. Where reputation can be reset cheaply, behaviour drifts towards exploitation; where it cannot, cooperation is more stable.

What does this mean for ordinary citizens? On the optimistic side, it suggests that we may have more leverage than we realise: the rules under which trust grows or collapses are not fixed by human nature, but tunable by design. On the more sobering side, the same insight implies that trust is a fragile achievement, dependent on environments most of us never directly choose. The next time someone tells you that 'people just are not as trustworthy as they used to be', the more interesting question may not be whether the claim is true, but what changed in the rules of the games we now play with each other.

Question 1: What is the article's main idea?
options: A. Trust depends on the structure of repeated interactions and can be shaped by design.
B. Trust is purely emotional and cannot be analysed rationally.
C. All cooperation is selfishness in disguise.
D. Trust between humans is impossible to measure.
questions.explanation: 全文围绕 'repeated interaction' 与 'reputation systems / rules ... tunable by design'。其余 B/C/D 走极端。

Online marketplaces have learned, sometimes painfully, that visible reputation systems shift behaviour by changing how often a single bad action will be remembered in future ones. Where reputation can be reset cheaply, behaviour drifts towards exploitation; where it cannot, cooperation is more stable.

What does this mean for ordinary citizens? On the optimistic side, it suggests that we may have more leverage than we realise: the rules under which trust grows or collapses are not fixed by human nature, but tunable by design. On the more sobering side, the same insight implies that trust is a fragile achievement, dependent on environments most of us never directly choose. The next time someone tells you that 'people just are not as trustworthy as they used to be', the more interesting question may not be whether the claim is true, but what changed in the rules of the games we now play with each other.

options: A. Paragraph 4 extends the logic of paragraph 3 to institutions.
B. Paragraph 4 refutes the findings of paragraph 3.
C. Paragraph 4 introduces an unrelated economic theory.
D. Paragraph 4 is a personal anecdote.
questions.explanation: 段 4 第一句 'The same mathematics applies, in messier form, to whole institutions' — 直接延伸。

questions.content:
The Mathematics of Trust

Most accounts of human cooperation focus on the warmer side of the question: kindness, fellow-feeling, the bonds of family or community. Recent research, while not denying these, has shifted attention to something colder. A great deal of what looks like trust between people may, on close examination, be the result of repeated calculation in environments shaped to make cooperation pay.

The starting point of this research is a simple game played in laboratories around the world. Two strangers are paired and given small sums of money. Each can choose, repeatedly, either to share or to keep. Sharing benefits the partner more than it costs you, but only if the partner also shares in return. Long ago, the game was thought to expose human selfishness; with each round, players were predicted to defect. Real human players, however, cooperate far more often than this prediction suggests, especially when they expect to interact again.

That last condition — repeated interaction — turns out to be doing much of the work. Once players know they will face each other again, simple strategies that punish past defection and reward past cooperation outperform almost everything else over time. These strategies, no more complex than 'do unto others as they last did unto you', emerge naturally in groups that play often. They look, on the surface, very much like trust.

The same mathematics applies, in messier form, to whole institutions. Banks that expect to lend to customers many times over their lifetimes behave differently from banks that maximise quarterly fees on one-off transactions. Online marketplaces have learned, sometimes painfully, that visible reputation systems shift behaviour by changing how often a single bad action will be remembered in future ones. Where reputation can be reset cheaply, behaviour drifts towards exploitation; where it cannot, cooperation is more stable.

What does this mean for ordinary citizens? On the optimistic side, it suggests that we may have more leverage than we realise: the rules under which trust grows or collapses are not fixed by human nature, but tunable by design. On the more sobering side, the same insight implies that trust is a fragile achievement, dependent on environments most of us never directly choose. The next time someone tells you that 'people just are not as trustworthy as they used to be', the more interesting question may not be whether the claim is true, but what changed in the rules of the games we now play with each other.

Question 4: According to the article, what kind of online marketplace is most likely to encourage cooperation?
options: A. One in which a user's reputation cannot easily be reset.
B. One in which all transactions are anonymous.
C. One in which reviews are deleted weekly.
D. One in which good and bad behaviour are equally rewarded.
questions.explanation: 段 4 'where it cannot [be reset cheaply], cooperation is more stable'。

Question 5: What does the writer suggest about claims that people are 'less trustworthy than they used to be'?

B. Such claims should be accepted without further inquiry.
C. Such claims are mathematically impossible.
D. Such claims prove that trust never existed.

questions.content:
The Self-Help Paradox

Walk into any large bookshop and you will see, somewhere near the entrance, a long shelf of books promising to make you happier, more focused, more productive, or — more cautiously — at least less anxious. The titles tend to be short, the subtitles long, and the covers are designed in colours selected by people who know exactly which shade of blue is associated, in shoppers' minds, with calm. The books sell, year after year, in extraordinary numbers.

It is fashionable to dismiss the genre as harmless nonsense, but harmlessness is not quite the right word. A book that genuinely helped its readers would, by definition, reduce demand for further books on the same problem. A book that does not help, but feels for a few weeks as if it might, is in a strange way better suited to the market. The customer remains, gently, a customer.

This is not necessarily a conspiracy. Most of the well-meaning authors I have spoken to are sincerely persuaded that they have something useful to share. The genre's structure, however, rewards a particular kind of incomplete success. Advice that is both true and obvious — eat reasonably, sleep enough, move every day, treat people decently — does not, on its own, sustain a 240-page narrative. So the books drape these unglamorous truths in the latest scientific costume, until the reader feels they have learned something genuinely new.

Cynicism here is easy and not very productive. There are excellent books in the broader category — careful, modest, properly footnoted. They are not always the bestsellers. The bestseller, in self-help as elsewhere, tends to combine a memorable single idea with the promise of more or less immediate transformation. Modest books are often what readers return to. Memorable books are often what they buy first.

The result is a kind of soft trap. Readers, understandably, want change to feel possible. Publishers, understandably, want books to sell. Authors, understandably, want their work read. Nobody is acting badly, and yet the system as a whole rewards the brilliantly packaged surface idea over the slower, less photogenic work. Perhaps the best advice for any reader entering that shelf for the first time is not to avoid it, but to remember that the books you keep returning to over the years are often very different from the ones you carry to the till.

Question 1: What is ironic about the writer's description of self-help books in paragraph 2?
options: A. A book that truly helped readers would actually reduce demand for more such books.
B. Self-help books are written by people who do not read.
C. The books are too short to be useful.
D. Bookshops never sell self-help books.
questions.explanation: 段 2 'A book that genuinely helped ... would, by definition, reduce demand' — 内在矛盾即讽刺。

questions.audio_text: Good afternoon to all members of Westbrook Public Library. Please listen carefully to the following announcements concerning two recent changes. From the first of next month, our weekday opening hours will be shortened by one hour: the library will close at half past six rather than seven thirty. Saturday and Sunday hours remain unchanged. Secondly, our automatic renewal policy will end. From now on, members must renew borrowed items either online through their account or in person at the front desk before the due date. Items not renewed will accumulate the standard daily charge. We apologise for any inconvenience and thank you for your understanding.

B: Honestly, I keep going back and forth. The university swimming camp looks more serious — daily early sessions and proper coaching — but the city sports academy is shorter and includes a couple of weekends free.
A: Well, what matters more to you this year? Pushing your performance, or having time for family stuff?
B: That is exactly the question I keep avoiding. Last summer I burnt out a bit doing too much. I think I need progress, but not at the cost of being miserable by August.

What is the speaker's main message?
options: A. Volunteer experience can be valuable for university applications when chosen carefully.
B. Universities accept students based purely on volunteer hours.
C. All students must volunteer at hospitals.
D. Volunteering wastes time better spent on academic study.
questions.explanation: 讲座主旨：志愿活动有价值但要谨慎选择。其余选项过度或相反。
questions.audio_text: Welcome, everyone. I am here today to talk about the role of volunteer experience in your university applications. There is a common misconception that admissions officers count volunteer hours like points on a scoreboard. They do not. What they look for is evidence of genuine, sustained engagement with a cause that says something about who you are. A single deep year supporting one local charity, with clear examples of what you contributed, is almost always stronger than thirty disconnected one-off events. Choose carefully, document carefully, reflect carefully — and remember that the experience you are buying is, ultimately, your own growth, not just a line on the application form.

What is the speaker's view on home-cooked meals?
options: A. She values them but acknowledges the time pressure on busy families.
B. She believes everyone must cook from scratch every night.
C. She thinks ready meals are nutritionally identical to home cooking.
D. She has given up on home cooking entirely.
questions.explanation: 讲者称重要但理解时间压力 — 平衡观点。
questions.audio_text: Good evening. Tonight I want to talk briefly about home cooking — but not in the lecturing tone the topic usually attracts. Yes, food made at home tends to contain less salt, less sugar, and more vegetables than its packaged equivalents. Yes, eating around a table is good for families. None of that is news. What is more interesting, and less often discussed, is how unrealistic the standard advice can sound to a single parent finishing a long shift. So my honest position is this: home cooking matters and is worth defending; but the way to defend it is not to shame busy households into impossible standards. Two reasonable home-cooked meals a week, treated as a small ritual rather than a moral test, may do more good than a guilt-driven attempt at seven.
