questions.explanation: 他承认 deep work 优势，但担心 junior staff 学习损失。其余三项过极。
questions.audio_text:
A: After three years of mixed remote and office working, what is your honest view?
B: My view is more nuanced than it used to be. For deep individual work — writing reports, focused analysis — I am genuinely more productive at home. The numbers back this up.
A: Sounds fully positive.
B: That is only half. The part that worries me is the junior staff. Two or three of our recent graduates have told me they feel they are not learning the small things that used to be picked up by overhearing experienced colleagues handle a tricky call.
A: So the question is no longer about productivity but about apprenticeship.
B: Yes. And we have not solved it. We have only postponed thinking about it.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What is the speaker's attitude towards online reviews?
options: A. Skeptical — useful only if read with care for systematic biases.
B. Trusting — believes most reviews accurately reflect the product.
C. Hostile — believes all reviews should be banned.
D. Indifferent — believes reviews have no influence on anyone.
questions.explanation: 讲者说有用但要警惕系统性偏差。
questions.audio_text: Tonight, a short piece on online reviews. The standard advice — read the one-stars and the five-stars, then decide — sounds wise but it leaves out something important: the reviewers themselves are not a representative sample. People who write reviews tend to be either unusually delighted or unusually furious. The quiet middle, which is most of us most of the time, rarely posts. That does not mean the reviews are useless. It means they describe the extremes of an experience reasonably well, and the typical experience rather poorly. Read with that filter, they remain a tool. Read without it, they can mislead you into thinking the world is more dramatic than it actually is.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

How does the woman feel about giving up social media?
options: A. Mostly positive but occasionally lonely about missing certain conversations.
B. Completely regretful and planning to return immediately.
C. Indifferent — she did not really use it before.
D. Hostile to anyone who still uses it.
questions.explanation: 她大体满意但偶尔孤独，并不极端否定他人。
questions.audio_text:
A: It has been six months since you deleted the apps. Any regrets?
B: Mostly no. My sleep is better, I read again, my actual conversations are deeper because I am not skimming everyone's life over breakfast.
A: So an unqualified success?
B: Not quite. There are evenings — usually after a long quiet day — when I genuinely miss the chatter, especially around things like film releases or politics, where everyone seems to have processed an event before I have heard about it.
A: But it has not made you reinstall.
B: Not yet. The trade is still on my side. Whether that lasts another six months, I have no idea.
A: Fair enough.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What is the speaker's main view on self-driving cars?
options: A. Practical questions of liability matter more than the technology itself.
B. The technology will never work in any city.
C. Self-driving cars are already perfect and need no further work.
D. Insurance companies have already solved every problem.
questions.explanation: 讲者强调 liability 法律问题比技术更难解决。
questions.audio_text: There is a tendency, when self-driving cars come up at conferences, to focus almost entirely on the technology — the cameras, the sensors, the software. That part is genuinely impressive and improving every year. The harder problem, in my opinion, is not technical at all. When a self-driving car causes harm — and statistically, eventually, one will — who is liable? The owner who pressed the button? The manufacturer of the car? The supplier of the perception software? The agency that approved the model for road use? Until liability is settled, deployment in dense cities will remain partial and patchy. The tail will not catch up with the dog because the tail is being held back by an unanswered legal question.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What do all three speakers agree on?
options: A. That the school cafeteria menu needs more vegetarian options.
B. That cafeteria food is currently perfect.
C. That all students should bring their own lunch.
D. That the cafeteria should close on Mondays.
questions.explanation: 三人都说素食选择不够 — 唯一一致点。
questions.audio_text:
A: I really think the cafeteria menu is too meat-heavy this term.
B: I would not go that far, but I do agree they have only one vegetarian dish per day, and it is usually the same kind of pasta.
C: Yes, even for those of us who are not strictly vegetarian, sometimes we just want a non-meat option that is interesting.
A: Exactly. A bean stew, a tofu curry, something with proper seasoning.
B: Right. I would happily eat that twice a week.
C: So we are in agreement — the food itself is fine, but the variety on the vegetarian side is the issue.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

Which speaker most strongly opposes the proposed school uniform change?
options: D. None of them — they all support it
questions.explanation: C 明确强烈反对；A 支持；B 含糊。
questions.audio_text:
A: I think the new uniform proposal is sensible. The current jacket is too warm and too expensive.
B: I see your point on the jacket, but changing the whole uniform feels excessive. Maybe just an alternative jacket would be enough.
C: I have to disagree with both of you. Switching uniforms in the middle of the school year creates real costs for families, and most students are perfectly happy with what we have. I am firmly against it.
A: But families will save money in the long run.
C: That is a guess. The immediate cost is certain. I cannot support it.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What does the family decide to do for the trip?
options: A. Drive instead of taking the train, because of cost and luggage.
C. Take the train and pay extra for luggage.
D. Fly to save time despite the cost.
questions.explanation: 三人最终选择开车（讨论了三选项），结合行李 + 价格。
questions.audio_text:
A (Father): So options for the trip: train, plane, or drive. Train is comfortable but the four of us with all the camping gear is going to be over a hundred and fifty pounds in baggage alone.
B (Mother): That is what worries me. The basic ticket looks cheap until you add bikes and the big bag.
C (Teenage daughter): Plane is out, right? It is too short a distance.
A: Yes, and the train station to the campsite still needs a taxi.
B: So we drive.
C: I am fine with driving as long as we stop somewhere for lunch.
A: Agreed, we drive. I will plan the route tonight.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What is the main problem the team is trying to solve?
options: A. Their event website crashed and they need a quick recovery plan.
B. Their team has lost a major sponsor.
C. Their event location has been changed by the venue.
D. Their main speaker has cancelled the keynote.
questions.explanation: 三人讨论网站宕机和恢复计划。其余三项与对话无关。
questions.audio_text:
A: Right, urgent meeting. The event registration site went down at three this afternoon and we still cannot bring it back up. We have ninety minutes before evening hits and people start trying to register after work.
B: I have already contacted the hosting team. Their estimate is forty minutes for full recovery, but they cannot guarantee it.
C: In the meantime, can we put up a single static page with a Google form for registrations? We feed those into the database afterwards.
A: Good. Do that immediately. B, post a clear update on social media: not a panic, just 'site temporarily down, please use this form'.
B: On it.
C: I will write the form right now.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

Which of the three speakers is most uncertain about the plan?
options: D. None — all are equally uncertain
questions.explanation: B 多次表示 'not sure' / 'we need more data'，A 决断，C 支持。
questions.audio_text:
A: We move ahead with the launch in October.
B: I am genuinely not sure that is enough time. Marketing have only signed off on half the materials, and the user testing data we have is from a sample of barely sixty people.
C: I share some of B's concerns about testing, but I think a delay would cost us more than another rushed quarter would.
A: Exactly. October. We can patch in November.
B: I would push back. We need more data before we commit publicly. I would rather a controlled November launch than an October launch we have to defend.
C: Fair, but I lean A's way.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What do the three friends finally decide to do for the weekend?
options: A. Visit an art exhibition together on Saturday afternoon.
B. Stay home because the weather is bad.
C. Drive to the seaside for a picnic.
D. Go to a concert in the city centre.
questions.explanation: 经过提议-否决，最终决定 Saturday afternoon 看艺术展。其余被否或未选。
questions.audio_text:
A: What about a hike on Saturday morning?
B: Have you seen the forecast? Heavy rain all weekend.
C: Indoor activity then. The new photography exhibition at the gallery — anyone interested?
A: I would go. They had good reviews in the paper.
B: I am not a huge gallery person but I have heard this one is genuinely interesting.
C: Saturday afternoon, two o'clock entry?
A: Works for me.
B: Yes, two o'clock is fine. I will buy our tickets online tonight to skip the queue.
C: Perfect. Gallery on Saturday it is.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What is the tutor's main suggestion to the student?
options: A. To narrow the essay question and add a clearer central argument.
B. To rewrite the entire draft from scratch.
C. To submit the essay without changes.
D. To choose a completely new topic.
questions.explanation: 导师建议缩小问题范围 + 加明确论点；并未要求重写或换题。
questions.audio_text:
A (Tutor): I have read your draft. There is a great deal of good reading here, but the essay is trying to do too many things at once.
B (Student): I was worried about that. I went down too many rabbit holes.
A: My main suggestion is not to rewrite from scratch — there is solid material — but to narrow the question. At the moment the title is asking about three centuries of policy. Pick one clear claim that runs from your introduction through your conclusion, and prune anything that does not directly serve it.
B: So less width, more depth.
A: Exactly. And add an explicit thesis sentence in the introduction, not a vague signpost. That alone will tighten about half of the section issues.
B: Got it. I will redraft the introduction tonight and rebuild the structure around it.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What does the professor recommend before the student begins fieldwork?
options: A. To pilot the survey on a small group and revise the unclear questions.
B. To skip the literature review.
C. To collect all data first and analyse later.
D. To switch to a quantitative method.
questions.explanation: 教授强调先小规模 pilot 测试，找出题目不清楚的地方修改。
questions.audio_text:
A (Student): I am ready to start handing the surveys out next week. I have a list of fifty respondents.
B (Professor): Before you do, I strongly recommend a pilot. Print just five copies, give them to people similar to your final sample, and watch them complete the survey. You will be surprised how often a question that seems clear to you confuses the actual reader.
A: Five copies feels low. Should it be more?
B: For a pilot, no. Five is enough to expose the worst ambiguities. Larger pilots become essentially small studies and consume time you need for the real fieldwork. Revise based on what you observe, then go to the full fifty.
A: Got it. Pilot Monday, revise Tuesday, fieldwork from Wednesday.
B: Sensible.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

How does the supervisor evaluate the student's literature review?
options: A. Comprehensive but lacking critical engagement with the sources.
B. Too short and missing key authors.
C. Excellent and ready for submission.
D. Off-topic and needing a complete restart.
questions.explanation: 导师称范围齐全但批判分析不足；并非太短或重启。
questions.audio_text:
A (Supervisor): The literature review is impressively comprehensive — you have covered every major author I would expect to see and several I would not have thought of.
B (Student): Thanks. I worked on it most of August.
A: Now, here is the harder feedback. At the moment it reads like a series of careful summaries. Each paragraph correctly describes what someone said. What it does not yet do, in most paragraphs, is tell me what you make of it. Where do these authors disagree? Whose argument do you find most persuasive, and why? Whose evidence is weakest?
B: So it is a critical-engagement issue, not a coverage issue.
A: Exactly. Coverage is solid. Take a week to add evaluative sentences — your own voice — to each section.
B: Will do.

questions.content:
Listen and choose the correct answer.

What is the main reason the seminar group's discussion stalled?
options: A. Most participants had not done the assigned reading in time.
B. The room was too noisy.
C. The tutor refused to attend.
D. The seminar topic was changed last minute.
questions.explanation: 讨论被卡是因大多数没读 reading；其余三项与对话不符。
questions.audio_text:
A: I am sorry today's seminar was so flat. I had hoped for more discussion.
B: I think only three of us had actually finished the reading, to be honest. The rest were skim-reading during the first ten minutes.
A: Yes, that was the problem. There is no way to lead a serious discussion of a complex paper if most of the room has only read the abstract.
B: It might help if next week's reading is shorter, or if we split it into sections and assign different students to lead each.
A: I had not thought of dividing it. Let me take that to the staff and we will trial it for the next two seminars.
B: Sounds good — I think people will engage more if their part is clearly theirs.

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning of the original sentence.

Original: 'I started learning the violin five years ago.'
options: A. I have been learning the violin for five years.
B. I had been learning the violin for five years.
C. I am learning the violin since five years.
D. I will be learning the violin in five years.
questions.explanation: started X years ago = have been V-ing for X years（现在完成进行）。其余时态错或时间介词错。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'It is unlikely that he will arrive on time.'
options: A. He is unlikely to arrive on time.
B. He must arrive on time.
C. He will likely not arrive on time, although he definitely will.
D. It is necessary that he arrives on time.
questions.explanation: It is unlikely that S V → S is unlikely to V（标准 it-shift）。其余 B/C 自相矛盾或反义；D 改成必要。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'Although she was tired, she finished the project.'
options: A. Despite being tired, she finished the project.
B. Because she was tired, she finished the project.
C. If she had been tired, she would have finished the project.
D. She finished the project so that she was tired.
questions.explanation: Although + 句 = Despite + V-ing / N（让步介词替换）。B 把因果反转；C 是 type 3 假设；D 是结果状语，意思错。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'We should have left earlier.'
options: A. We didn't leave early enough — and now we regret it.
B. We will leave earlier next time, definitely.
C. We can leave earlier whenever we want.
D. We left earlier, which was correct.
questions.explanation: should have + p.p. = 当时本应做但没做（含遗憾）。B 是未来计划；C 是能力；D 与原句相反。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'Nobody told me about the change.'
options: A. I was not told about the change by anyone.
B. I told nobody about the change.
C. Nobody knew about the change.
D. I did not change anyone.
questions.explanation: 主动→被动：Nobody told me → I was not told by anyone（'by anyone' 表 'nobody' 的被动等价）。其余三项含义改变。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'She insisted on paying the bill.'
options: A. She insisted that she pay the bill.
B. She refused to pay the bill.
C. She forgot to pay the bill.
D. She apologised for paying the bill.
questions.explanation: insist on + V-ing = insist that S + (should) + V（虚拟 mandative）。其余三项改了意思。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'If I had known about the meeting, I would have attended.'
options: A. Had I known about the meeting, I would have attended.
B. If I knew about the meeting, I would attend.
C. If I will know about the meeting, I will attend.
D. I attended the meeting because I knew.
questions.explanation: Type 3 倒装：Had + S + p.p. = If + S + had + p.p.。其余 B 是 type 2；C 时态错；D 与虚拟相反。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'They didn't recognise me because I had grown a beard.'
options: A. Having grown a beard, I was not recognised by them.
B. Although I had grown a beard, they recognised me.
C. I grew a beard because they did not recognise me.
D. They recognised me, having grown a beard.
questions.explanation: perfect participle (Having + p.p.) + 被动主句 = 因长胡子而未被认出。B/C 因果颠倒；D 主语逻辑错（grown 主语应是 I）。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'It was the noise that woke me up at 3 a.m.'
options: A. What woke me up at 3 a.m. was the noise.
B. I woke up because the noise was at 3 a.m.
C. It was 3 a.m. when the noise woke up.
D. The noise that I woke up was at 3 a.m.
questions.explanation: It-cleft → What-cleft 等价改写：It was X that... = What was... X / What... was X。其余三项语法错或意思变。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'She rarely complains about her workload.'
options: A. Rarely does she complain about her workload.
B. She is rarely about her workload.
C. She does not complain rarely about her workload.
D. Rarely she complains about her workload.
questions.explanation: Rarely 置句首引发倒装：Rarely + does + S + V。B 缺动词；C 双重否定意思反；D 缺倒装结构。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'You can come in only if you have an appointment.'
options: A. Only if you have an appointment can you come in.
B. You only come in if you have an appointment certainly.
C. If you come in, you have an appointment only.
D. Only you can come in, if appointment yes.
questions.explanation: Only if + 子句 → 主句倒装：Only if + clause + auxiliary + S + V。其余三项语法/意思错。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'He is so proud that he never asks for help.'
options: A. He is too proud to ask for help.
B. He asks for help because he is proud.
C. He is not proud, so he never asks.
D. Despite his pride, he asks for help.
questions.explanation: so X that 否定/never V → too X to V。其余三项与原句意相反或不连贯。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'I prefer reading books to watching films.'
options: A. I would rather read books than watch films.
B. I prefer to watch films rather than read books.
C. I have read books and not watched films.
D. Reading books is harder than watching films.
questions.explanation: prefer + V-ing to V-ing = would rather + bare-V than bare-V。其余三项意思错。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'They did not let us into the building.'
options: A. We were not allowed into the building.
B. We were not letting them into the building.
C. We did not allow them into the building.
D. They were not in the building.
questions.explanation: let sb in (主动) → be allowed in (被动 + allow 替 let 因 let 被动罕见)。其余三项主宾或意思错。

questions.content:
Choose the rewrite that best preserves the meaning.

Original: 'It has been a long time since we last met.'
options: A. We have not met for a long time.
B. We have met for a long time recently.
C. We met long ago, but not since.
D. We did not meet a long time ago.
questions.explanation: It has been + 时长 + since (last) + 过去时 = S + have not + p.p. + for + 时长。B/C/D 时态或意思都不对（C 双重否定 awkward）。

questions.content:
Read the paragraph. Choose the sentence that best fits in the gap [1].

Online learning has expanded enormously over the past decade. [1] Critics, however, point out that completion rates for these courses remain disappointingly low, especially for learners without prior university experience.

Which sentence best fits gap [1]?
options: A. Free platforms now offer thousands of courses to anyone with an internet connection.
B. Most universities will close down by 2030.
C. Pencils were invented in the seventeenth century.
D. The price of bread has risen sharply this year.
questions.explanation: A 衔接 'expanded' 主题（具体例子：免费课程数千门），并自然引出 'critics 完成率低' 的转折。其余无关。

questions.content:
Choose the sentence that best fits the gap [1].

The city council announced a new bicycle-sharing scheme last month. [1] Despite this, ridership in the first three weeks has exceeded all forecasts, and several rental docks are routinely empty by 9 a.m.

Which sentence best fits gap [1]?
options: A. Local critics initially predicted that the bikes would mostly be stolen or vandalised.
B. The mayor announced his retirement during the same press conference.
C. Most residents prefer cars to any form of public transport.
D. Bicycle helmets were patented in 1880.
questions.explanation: A 与 'Despite this, ridership exceeded forecasts' 形成转折前提（批评者预言失败 → 实际超预期）。其余无逻辑衔接。

questions.content:
Choose the sentence that best fits the gap [1].

Researchers have long known that birdsong varies between regions. [1] By comparing the songs of birds in adjacent valleys, scientists can now identify subtle dialects that change measurably every few kilometres.

Which sentence best fits gap [1]?
options: A. More recently, advances in audio recording have made it possible to study these variations in much greater detail.
B. Most birds eat seeds and insects.
C. Bird-watching is a popular hobby in many countries.
D. Songbirds typically migrate in the autumn.
questions.explanation: A 衔接前句 (long known variation) 和后句 (now identify subtle dialects)：技术进步推动 detail。其余三项是无关一般事实。

questions.content:
Choose the sentence that best fits the gap [1].

When a new restaurant opens, marketing teams often focus on a memorable opening week. [1] In the long term, however, the steady quality of an unremarkable Wednesday matters more than any launch event.

Which sentence best fits gap [1]?
options: A. Special discounts and celebrity guests can produce striking photographs and a flood of social-media posts.
B. Tomatoes are technically a fruit.
C. The first restaurants in Europe appeared in eighteenth-century Paris.
D. Most chefs use stainless-steel knives.
questions.explanation: A 是 marketing 的具体例子（折扣 + 名人 + 照片）。后句对比 'long term steady quality matters more'。

questions.content:
Choose the sentence that best fits the gap [1].

The library plans to extend its weekend hours starting next month. [1] Funding for the additional staff and lighting will come from a small surcharge on overdue items, which the council approved at its last meeting.

Which sentence best fits gap [1]?
options: A. This decision follows months of consultation with regular users, many of whom requested longer Saturday access.
B. Books are usually arranged on shelves by genre.
C. Some libraries have cafés inside them.
D. Reading is a popular activity in many cities.
questions.explanation: A 解释 'extend its weekend hours' 决策来源（用户 consultation），并自然引出 funding 段。
